_doc: The YOLO desk ledger. One defined-risk intraday options idea per morning brief, keyed to the day's levels (gamma walls, flip, event triggers). PAPER ONLY, lottery-ticket framing — but analyzed and scored with full seriousness. Morning run appends the day's trade (status: open). Close run resolves it against the actual tape: did the trigger fire, did it reach target or invalidation, honest payoff estimate. Statuses: open | win | loss | no-trigger | expired. Resolved trades need a resolution_note with the mechanism. 'No clean setup, desk is flat' is a valid morning outcome — don't force a trade; flat days are not logged.

scorecard.notes: 2026-06-25: SIXTH no-trigger (the bullish 7,475c reclaim card) — and this time the stand-aside AVOIDED A LOSS. The card required SPX to RECLAIM 7,450 and HOLD it ~30 min (the hold-filter separating a gap-and-go from a gap-and-fade). What happened: the +2.4% Nasdaq-futures gap FADED into the cash open — SPX opened 7,404.91, ran only to 7,419.08 (~31 pts short of the 7,450 trigger), got rejected there and faded to a 7,323.50 low before closing 7,357.49. No reclaim of 7,450, no hold, no trade — the desk stood aside exactly as the card specified. Unlike June 10/24 (no-triggers that MISSED winners), this one avoided a LOSS: the bullish thesis was WRONG (no gap-and-go; the index never cleared the flip and closed below its open), so a 7,475 call bought on a loose entry would have expired worthless. The hold-filter did its job — it demanded confirmation the gap never delivered, and the most bullish catalyst stack of the month (soft PCE + MU blowout + QCOM) STILL couldn't carry the flip because the mega-cap memory-cost selloff capped it. Mechanism footnote: a premarket futures gap is not a cash-session reclaim; keying the long to a held break of the flip (not to the gap) correctly kept the desk out of a fade. This is the bullish mirror of the lesson the week's fades taught — no confirmation, no trade, in either direction. Desk record 1-2-6 (6 stand-asides; 4 avoided losses, 2 missed winners). PRIOR — 2026-06-24: FIFTH no-trigger (fade-the-relief-rally, 7,400 put) — and the first one where the discipline COST a winner rather than avoiding a loss. The card required SPX to POKE the 7,440-7,450 flip zone in the first ~90 min and then fail to hold it; the day's HIGH was only 7,428.06 — it rejected ~12-22 pts BELOW the trigger zone and never reached it. No poke into 7,440+, no trade, desk stood aside. But the directional thesis was dead RIGHT: SPX got sold at 7,428, faded all afternoon, and closed 7,358.22 — a 7,400 put bought at the open would have finished ~42 pts ITM, a clear win. The miss was trigger PLACEMENT: we keyed the fade to a poke into the round 7,450 flip when the tape was actually respecting the lower 7,424-7,428 broken-shelf as resistance (it had rejected there June 23 too). The rejection came at the level the tape cared about, not the level on the card. New lesson distilled: in a sub-flip negative-gamma tape, set the fade trigger at the resistance the tape is ACTUALLY respecting (the recent rejection high / broken shelf), not at the round flip number above it — or you watch your thesis pay from the sidelines. Desk record 1-2-5 (5 stand-asides; 3 avoided losses, 2 now missed winners — June 10 and June 24). PRIOR — 2026-06-23: FOURTH no-trigger (short-gamma flush, 7,350 put) — the desk's origin trade got its textbook setup (real catalyst: a global memory rout; thin cushion: net GEX flipped negative -$49.4B; open below the 7,446 flip) and STILL didn't pay. SPX dripped to 7,347.60 (a 4-pt poke below the 7,352 trigger, AT the 7,350 put wall) then REVERSED 76 pts to 7,424.17 and closed 7,365.46 — a gap-and-reverse, no sustained momentum break to the 7,300 target. Per the card's own terms (demand a real break after the opening swing, not a tag) the trade never triggered; and read generously, the rally through 7,400 would have stopped any entry out for a loss, so discipline AVOIDED a loss for the 4th time (mirror of June 18). Mechanism: the 7,350 put wall caught the flush and an oversold tape bounced off it — the wall is the magnet/exit on the way down, but a fast flush into it can stall and reverse THERE. Desk record 1-2-4 (4 correct stand-asides now). PRIOR — 2026-06-18: third no-trigger (fade-the-reclaim 7,450 put) — SPX tagged the 7,510 broken floor and PINNED at the 7,500 call wall rather than rejecting lower, so the fade never set up; UNLIKE June 10/16, this time the directional thesis was wrong (no fade), so the trigger discipline avoided a LOSS rather than missing a winner. Desk record 1-2-3. Seeded 2026-06-09 with the desk's origin trade. 2026-06-10: first no-trigger (CPI double-key failed soft; flush came on the war anyway). 2026-06-11: first loss — direction right, wall never tagged, 0DTE expired 6 points OTM; time-stop lesson added. Week-1 record after Friday June 12 (flat, by design): 1-1-1. 2026-06-15: second loss — peace-gap long, trigger fired, squeeze ran to 7,577.92 (tagged the 7,575 strike ~3pts ITM) then stalled at the 7,555 call wall and faded to close 7,554.29; target (7,590-7,600) was set ABOVE the dominant call wall, so the rally died at the wall below the target. New lesson: in positive-gamma, set the target AT the dominant wall, not beyond it. 2026-06-16: second no-trigger — the corrective FADE card (7,575 put, fade a record-poke) required SPX to poke the 7,590-7,600 zone first; the high was only 7,564.96, so the poke never came and the desk stood aside. Honest note: the fade thesis was directionally RIGHT (SPX faded to close -0.57% at 7,511.35, below the 7,555 pin), but the entry trigger never set up — discipline held (no improvised entry without the trigger). Desk record: 1-2-2. Pattern: the desk has now correctly STOOD ASIDE twice when its level-trigger didn't fire, even when the directional thesis paid — the process (lesson: no-trigger = no trade) is holding.

trades.thesis: Short-gamma regime (SPX below the 7,440 flip): once a flush starts, dealer hedging accelerates it toward the put wall at 7,300. The wall is the magnet and the target.
trades.trigger: Iran-headline flush breaking SPX lower with momentum while below the gamma flip
trades.invalidation: SPX reclaiming the morning range (flush failed)
trades.resolution_note: Origin trade, taken live (paper) by the curator. SPX flushed from the open to 7,297 by midday — the put wall tagged within 3 points — before the afternoon recovery to 7,387. The 7,300 puts captured the full leg down. Mechanism lesson: in a short-gamma tape the put wall is the TARGET of a flush, not the entry signal; the entry is the break of momentum below the flip. Also note the exit discipline this implies — the wall is where you sell, because that's where hedging flows turn from tailwind to headwind (today they did, violently: +90 points off the wall into the close).

trades.thesis: CPI-day binary in a short-gamma tape (SPX below the ~7,440 flip, net GEX deeply negative). A hot core print (>0.3% m/m) puts a June 17 hike in play with futures already opening near the 7,355 near-dated hedging ledge; losing that ledge hands the move to dealer hedging flows, which accelerate it toward the 7,300 aggregate put wall. Wall is the magnet and the exit.
trades.trigger: Double-keyed: (1) May core CPI prints ABOVE 0.3% m/m at 8:30 ET, AND (2) SPX loses the 7,355 hedging ledge after the open. Core <=0.3% = no trade, desk stands down.
trades.target: Tag of the 7,300 put wall — exit at the wall, where hedging flows flip from tailwind to headwind.
trades.invalidation: SPX reclaiming 7,386 (June 9 close) after the trigger fires; no averaging down.
trades.resolution_note: Core printed 0.2% — key (1) of the double-keyed trigger failed, so by the card's own terms the trade never existed; the desk stood down at 8:31 and stayed flat. The uncomfortable part, logged honestly: the flush came anyway. SPX lost the 7,355 ledge, broke 7,300, and closed 7,266.99 — the 7,300 puts would have paid handsomely on the war/supercore selloff. The card keyed the trade to the wrong branch: it assumed a hot core was the only path to a flush on a day carrying TWO live binaries (CPI and an overnight missile exchange). Mechanism lesson (paired with the cpi-core-asymmetry autopsy): the LEVELS were right twice over — 7,355 broke first, the rolled-down put wall (7,265) caught the close within 2 points. Key future triggers to the levels with the catalyst as context, not to one catalyst. Discipline was correct even though the P&L would have been good: no-trigger means no trade, and improvising the same structure off a different catalyst intraday is how a scoreable desk becomes a mood.

trades.thesis: The flush trade run in reverse. Dealer positioning is the most amplifying of the quarter (net GEX -$59B/-$74B both feeds) and the machinery is direction-agnostic: an upside break above resistance forces short-gamma dealers to buy as the market rises, chasing the move toward the 7,420-7,430 near call wall the way Monday's selling chased the flush to 7,300. Futures gap to ~7,325 puts the 7,355 ledge within one push. Per the playbook lesson from June 10: the trigger is the LEVEL; PPI and Hormuz headlines are context, not keys.
trades.trigger: SPX reclaims 7,355 (the near-dated hedging ledge) after the 8:30 ET PPI print and holds it ~30 minutes, regardless of which catalyst drives it there.
trades.target: 7,420-7,430 near call wall / flip zone. Exit at the wall, where dealer hedging flips from tailwind to headwind.
trades.invalidation: SPX back below 7,325 (the opening gap area) after entry - failed reclaim, exit, no re-entry. If 7,265 breaks instead of 7,355, the desk stays flat: no improvised bearish card (June 10 lesson - process, not mood).
trades.resolution_note: Trigger fired cleanly: the open faded the hot PPI to 7,257 (the 7,355 reclaim wasn't immediate), then the strike-cancellation headlines drove SPX through 7,355 with momentum and it held far longer than the required 30 minutes — and per the June 10 lesson the entry keyed to the level even though the catalyst was Iran, not PPI. The squeeze mechanism worked exactly as drawn: dealers chased the move 155 points off the low. Then it stalled at 7,412.68 — EIGHT points short of the 7,420-7,430 target wall (tonight's recompute puts the wall at 7,395/flip 7,424, i.e. the magnet was nearer and weaker than the morning map showed) — and faded to close 7,394.30. The card's rules had no exit for 'almost': target not tagged, invalidation (7,325) never touched, so the 0DTE call rode to expiry and finished 6 points out of the money. Worthless. A trade that was directionally right, mechanically right, and up big intraday (the call was well ITM at 7,412 with hours left) returned -100% because the exit logic only knew about the wall. Mechanism lesson: walls are magnets, not promises — an 0DTE card needs a time-stop (e.g., exit at 3:30 ET if neither target nor invalidation has hit) because theta resolves 'neither' against the holder, always. Desk record: 1-1-1.

trades.thesis: The pin run in reverse. The US-Iran deal ('complete,' signing Friday) gapped futures +1.3%, opening SPX near 7,520-7,530 — ABOVE the 7,425-7,430 call wall that capped every rally last week. A gap through the call wall flips dealers from selling-rallies (long-gamma pin) to chasing the move; with no price memory between Friday's 7,456 high and the June 1 record at 7,599.96, the air pocket is the trade. Per playbook lesson 1, keyed to the level (reclaim/hold 7,456), with the peace gap as context.
trades.trigger: SPX reclaims and HOLDS 7,456 (Friday's high) for ~30 minutes after the open. The hold-filter is the whole point: it separates a real breakout from a gap-and-fade that prints at 9:30 and dies by 10. No hold = no trade, desk stands aside (buy-the-rumor-sell-the-news on a pre-paid peace is the live alternative).
trades.target: 7,590-7,600 record zone (June 1 ATH 7,599.96). Exit there — a fresh all-time high is where profit-taking and dealer hedging flip from tailwind to headwind, same as a put wall on the way down.
trades.invalidation: Back below 7,456 after the reclaim — failed breakout, out, no re-entry, no bearish improvisation on the fade (June 10 lesson: process, not mood). Hard time-stop at 3:30 ET if neither target nor invalidation has hit (June 11 lesson: theta resolves 'almost' against the holder).
trades.resolution_note: Trigger fired cleanly: the gap opened SPX near 7,520 and the day's LOW was 7,516.75 — it never traded below 7,456, so the reclaim-and-hold filter was satisfied from the open. The melt-up materialized exactly as drawn: dealers chased the move into the air pocket above last week's 7,430 ceiling, and SPX ran to 7,577.92 — which TAGGED the 7,575 strike (the call went ~3 points ITM at the peak). Then it stalled and faded to close 7,554.29, pinning to the 7,555 call wall (tonight's compute) — the target zone (7,590-7,600) was never reached, and the invalidation (7,456) was never threatened. By the 3:30 time-stop the index was already fading off the high toward the wall, so the time-stop exit landed the position near or below the strike; held to expiry it finished ~21 points OTM and worthless. Verdict: LOSS. This is the June 11 card almost beat-for-beat — direction right, trigger right, squeeze right, up nicely intraday, then stalled SHORT of the target wall and faded. The new, distinct lesson this time: the morning card set the TARGET (7,590-7,600 record zone) ABOVE the dominant call wall (7,555). In a deeply positive-gamma regime the call wall is where dealers SELL — the rally was always likely to die AT the wall, below a target placed beyond it. The June 11 fix (time-stop) was correctly in place and did its job (capped the bleed); the miss this time was target placement, not exit logic. Lesson distilled to playbook. Desk record: 1-2-1.

trades.thesis: The explicit correction of the desk's last two losses (June 11, June 15), both of which BOUGHT a melt-up and set the target one wall too far. Today flips it: SPX closed 7,554.29 pinned to the 7,555 call wall with net GEX at a cycle-high positive (+$80B/+$101B). In a deeply positive-gamma tape the call wall is where dealers SELL to stay hedged, so a gap into the 7,590-7,600 record zone is most likely to get leaned on, not extended — especially the day before a hawkish-Warsh Fed more likely to inject caution than fuel. The wall is a magnet on the way back down just as it caps on the way up; fade the poke, target the pin.
trades.trigger: SPX pokes the 7,590-7,600 record zone (June 1 ATH 7,599.96) in the first hour AND fails to hold it (~15-30 min rejection, not a clean break). No poke into the zone = no trade, desk stands aside. The hold-filter is the whole point: it separates a rejection at the wall from a real breakout.
trades.target: The 7,555 call-wall pin — exit there, where dealer hedging flips from headwind to tailwind for this position. The wall is the magnet on the way down.
trades.invalidation: SPX holds above 7,600 for ~30 minutes after the poke (the wall migrated up, the breakout is real) — stand aside, no fighting it, no bearish re-entry. Hard time-stop at 3:30 ET if neither target nor invalidation has hit (June 11 lesson: theta resolves 'almost' against the holder).
trades.resolution_note: NO-TRIGGER. The card required SPX to POKE the 7,590-7,600 record zone in the first hour and then fail to hold it — that was the entire entry filter. The morning's +0.5% futures pop faded before the cash open; SPX began near flat and the intraday HIGH was only 7,564.96, ~25 points shy of the 7,590 trigger. No poke, no trade — the desk stood aside, exactly as the card specified. The uncomfortable-but-honest part: the directional thesis was RIGHT. SPX faded all session on the chip-for-cyclicals rotation and closed -0.57% at 7,511.35 — BELOW the 7,555 pin the put was meant to target, and below the 7,575 strike, so a put bought at the open would have paid. But the card keyed entry to a record-poke-and-reject setup that never materialized; the actual move was a straight-line drift lower with no record test to fade. Process held: this is the second time (June 10 the first) the desk correctly declined a trade whose level-trigger didn't fire even though an improvised entry would have paid — which is the whole point of a scoreable desk (lesson: no-trigger = no trade, process not mood). Mechanism footnote: the net-GEX collapse (+$101B->+$13B) meant there was far less dealer selling to lean on at the 7,600 wall, so the gap-into-the-record setup the card was built to fade simply didn't have the long-gamma fuel to gap there in the first place. Desk record: 1-2-2.

trades.direction: bearish (fade the reclaim attempt at the broken floor)
trades.thesis: Yesterday's hawkish-Fed flush broke the 7,510 put wall and closed 7,420.10; this morning's relief gap (SPY +0.71%, QQQ +1.59% premarket, ~7,470 implied open) has to climb back over 7,510 to undo it. With net dealer gamma drained (~+$22B, no re-stack) there's no mechanical dip-buying engine behind the bounce — it's real money, not dealer hedging — and the Fed just removed the dovish surprise that could carry a reclaim. So a poke into the broken floor with no automatic tailwind and Fed speakers more likely to reinforce than walk back the dots is the textbook spot for a relief rally to get sold at resistance. Keyed to the level (7,510) per playbook lesson 1; this morning's bounce is context, not a key. Internally consistent with our house call 2026-06-17-no-reclaim-7510.
trades.trigger: SPX rallies into the 7,500-7,510 zone (yesterday's broken put wall, now overhead resistance) in the first ~90 minutes AND fails to hold it (15-30 min rejection, not a clean break above). No poke into 7,500+ = no trade, desk stands aside. A clean HOLD above 7,510 = no trade (that's our no-reclaim call breaking; stand aside, don't fight it).
trades.target: The 7,400-7,415 shelf where yesterday's selling came to rest and fresh hedges stacked. Exit there.
trades.invalidation: SPX holds above 7,510 for ~30 minutes after the poke (the reclaim is real — our thesis is wrong) — out, no bearish re-entry. Hard time-stop at 3:30 ET if neither target nor invalidation has hit (June 11 lesson: theta resolves 'almost' against the holder).
trades.resolution_note: NO-TRIGGER. The card required a poke into the 7,500-7,510 broken-floor zone in the first ~90 min AND a real rejection/fade (15-30 min turn lower, the thing you short into) — that was the entry filter. What actually happened: SPX gapped up, tagged the 7,510 floor (high 7,511.07), and then PINNED at the 7,500 call wall all session in a tight 42-point (0.57%) range, closing 7,500.58. There was no rejection to fade — the index didn't turn DOWN from 7,510, it sat just below it; the day's low (7,468) never came within 18 points of the 7,450 strike, let alone the 7,400-7,415 target. The relief rally got CONTAINED at the broken floor (positive-gamma re-stack), not SOLD off it, so the bearish setup the card was built to catch never presented. Honest reading, the uncomfortable mirror of June 10/16: those two no-triggers stood aside while the directional thesis paid; THIS time the directional thesis was WRONG (no fade lower — the market pinned), so the no-trigger discipline AVOIDED a loss rather than missing a winner. Read loosely as 'tagged 7,510, didn't break above,' the 7,450 put would have been entered and expired ~50 pts OTM and worthless — a loss. The trigger filter (demand a real rejection, not just a tag) saved the desk for the second time in a week. Mechanism footnote validated the morning caveat in reverse: the cushion re-stacked faster than the thin-gamma read implied, so 7,510 acted as a calm pin-ceiling, not a slice-through level. Desk record: 1-2-3.

trades.thesis: The desk's origin trade — the short-gamma flush — set up by a genuine catalyst meeting a thin cushion. Overnight global memory rout (Kospi -10% with a circuit-breaker halt, Samsung/SK Hynix -12%, ASML -5%, MU -8% pre-market) on a Dan Niles AI-'speed-bump' call + Broadcom caution + rate fears. S&P futures -1.3% imply an open near 7,370-7,380, BELOW the 7,446 zero-gamma flip, where dealer hedging amplifies instead of dampens. Net GEX was only +$8.8B (thin) into last night, so a real catalyst slices the wall the way the playbook (lessons 10/11) says it does. Below the flip there is no automatic buyer; the next magnets are 7,400 (first hedge shelf, likely gapped through), 7,352, then 7,300 — the prior aggregate put wall that caught two flushes within 2 pts June 9/10.
trades.trigger: SPX LOSES 7,352 with momentum after the first 30 minutes (not just gaps there — breaks below once the opening swing settles), regardless of which headline drives it (playbook lesson 1: key to the level, not the catalyst). A gap-down that immediately RECLAIMS 7,446 in the opening hour = no trade, desk stands aside (gap-and-reverse is the live alternative on a panic open).
trades.target: 7,300 — the prior aggregate put wall / session magnet. Exit there, where dealer hedging flips from tailwind to headwind (it did, violently, +90 pts off the wall on June 9).
trades.invalidation: SPX reclaims 7,400 (the broken hedge shelf) after entry — flush failed, out, no averaging down. Hard time-stop at 3:30 ET if neither target nor invalidation has hit (June 11 lesson: theta resolves 'almost' against the holder).
trades.resolution_note: NO-TRIGGER. The card required SPX to LOSE 7,352 WITH MOMENTUM after the first 30 minutes (not just gap there — 'breaks below once the opening swing settles') and run toward the 7,300 target. What actually happened: SPX gapped down and opened 7,366.51, dripped to a day-low of 7,347.60 — a ~4-point poke below the 7,352 trigger level, at/near the 7,350 put wall — and then REVERSED 76 points to 7,424.17 as bargain-hunters bid non-chip tech and defensives, closing 7,365.46 back above 7,352. There was no sustained momentum break toward 7,300 (the target was never within 47 points); instead the put wall caught the flush and the tape gap-and-reversed UP — the exact stand-aside alternative the card named ('a gap-down that immediately reclaims... = no trade'). The honest, uncomfortable read (4th time in this pattern): even taken GENEROUSLY as a trigger on the brief sub-7,352 tag, the subsequent rally through 7,400 (high 7,424.17) would have hit the invalidation and stopped the position out for a LOSS — the target (7,300) never came close. So for the 4th time the trigger discipline (demand a real momentum break, not a 4-point poke) AVOIDED a loss rather than missing a winner, mirroring June 18 almost exactly. Mechanism footnote: the desk got its 'perfect' origin-trade setup (real catalyst + thin cushion + open below the flip, net GEX now -$49.4B) and STILL didn't pay — because the 7,350 put wall acted as support/magnet and an oversold tape bounced off it hard. The wall is the exit/magnet on the way DOWN, but on a fast flush into it the same hedging that targets it can also stall and reverse the move there. Desk record: 1-2-4.

trades.direction: bearish (fade the relief rally at the flip)
trades.thesis: The relief-bounce fade, consistent with our logged no-reclaim-7450 call. After yesterday's -1.44% flush closed 7,365.46 below the ~7,450 zero-gamma flip (net GEX negative, ~-$49.4B = AMPLIFY mode), an overnight V-shaped Asia recovery (Kospi +3.3%; Samsung +6-9.8% on a planned ~$59B buyback; SK Hynix accelerating a $26B US ADR listing) plus MU +4% premarket has US futures modestly green (QQQ +0.46%, MAGS +0.29%), implying an open near 7,400 - recovering a slice of yesterday but short of the 7,450 line. The genuine catalyst (Micron's 2027 guide) is AFTER the close, so intraday the relief gap has to climb the flip on its own steam, and in a negative-gamma tape the flip is overhead resistance - the natural spot for an unfueled bounce to run out of gas and get sold. Keyed to the level (7,450) per playbook lesson 1; the buyback/relief is context, not a key.
trades.trigger: SPX rallies into the 7,440-7,450 flip zone in the first ~90 minutes AND fails to hold it (15-30 min rejection back below ~7,420, not a clean break). No poke into 7,440+ = no trade, desk stands aside. A clean HOLD above 7,450 for ~30 min = no trade (that is our no-reclaim-7450 call breaking - stand aside, do not fight it).
trades.target: The 7,350 put wall (yesterday's low 7,347.60, the floor on the chart), where dealer hedging flips from tailwind to headwind for a short. First checkpoint the 7,400 shelf.
trades.invalidation: SPX holds above 7,450 for ~30 min after the poke (the reclaim is real, thesis wrong) - out, no bearish re-entry. Hard time-stop at 3:30 ET - do NOT carry a directional 0DTE into Micron's after-close print; theta + event risk both resolve against the holder overnight.
trades.resolution_note: NO-TRIGGER — and the costliest one yet, because the thesis paid and we weren't on it. The card required SPX to POKE the 7,440-7,450 flip zone in the first ~90 min AND fail to hold it (rejection back below ~7,420). What happened: SPX opened 7,370.88, rallied to a HIGH of 7,428.06 — ~12-22 pts SHORT of the 7,440 trigger floor — got rejected there, and faded all afternoon to close 7,358.22 (-0.10%). The poke into 7,440+ never came, so by the card's terms the trade never existed; the desk stood aside. The uncomfortable part: the FADE itself was exactly right — the bounce died at resistance and a 7,400 put bought at the open would have closed ~42 pts ITM (a clean win). The error was trigger PLACEMENT, not direction: we set the entry at the round 7,450 flip when the tape was respecting the LOWER 7,424-7,428 broken-shelf/June-23-high as its real resistance (it had been rejected at ~7,424 the day before, too). The rejection came where the tape cared, ~15 pts under our trigger. This is the second time the no-trigger discipline missed a winner (June 10 the first) rather than avoiding a loss (June 18/23). Mechanism footnote: in a negative-gamma tape below the flip, the operative resistance is the most recent rejection high, not the round flip number above it — the flip is where dealers' behavior changes, but price can get sold well below it. Lesson distilled to playbook. Desk record: 1-2-5. NOTE: the time-stop rule (no 0DTE held into MU's AH print) was correctly built into the card and would have mattered had we been in — MU then blew out +12% AH, exactly the overnight event risk the rule exists to avoid.

trades.direction: bullish (gap through the flip, momentum continuation to the call wall)
trades.thesis: The bullish mirror of the fade we've run all week, and the one day the setup flips long because the catalyst is genuine and there is NO event risk left to duck (PCE released soft this morning, Micron reported last night). Three bullish catalysts landed overnight: MU's blowout (+17% premarket), Qualcomm's AI-data-center declaration + Meta as customer (+12-15% AH), and a SOFT core PCE (0.2% m/m / ~2.9% y/y, under the ~0.3%/3.3% feared). Nasdaq futures +2.4%, S&P +0.8% imply an open ~7,410-7,420, right under the ~7,450 zero-gamma flip that capped every bounce this week (yesterday's 7,428 high died beneath it). A gap THROUGH the flip on real news forces the same dealers who amplified this week's selling to chase the move UP; the next magnet is the 7,510 broken-floor-now-ceiling. Keyed to the level (reclaim/hold 7,450) per playbook lesson 1; the MU/QCOM/PCE trifecta is context, not the key. Note: if this works, our own house call 2026-06-23-no-reclaim-7450 grades WRONG today — we take that trade.
trades.trigger: SPX reclaims 7,450 (the zero-gamma flip) after the open and HOLDS it ~30 minutes. The hold-filter is the whole point: it separates a gap-and-go (real positive-gamma re-stack) from a gap-and-fade that prints at 9:30 and dies by 10. No hold above 7,450 = no trade, desk stands aside (a poke-and-reject back under ~7,430 is the live alternative — that is our no-reclaim call surviving, do not fight it with a bearish improvisation).
trades.target: The 7,510 broken-floor-now-ceiling / dominant call-wall zone, where dealer hedging flips from tailwind to headwind for a long. Exit AT the wall, not above it (lesson 8: in a re-stacked positive-gamma tape the wall is where the move dies). First checkpoint the round 7,500.
trades.invalidation: SPX back below ~7,440 after the reclaim — failed breakout, out, no bearish re-entry on the fade (June 10 lesson: process, not mood). Hard time-stop at 3:30 ET if neither target nor invalidation has hit (June 11 lesson: theta resolves 'almost' against the holder). No overnight carry needed — event risk is behind us today.
trades.resolution_note: NO-TRIGGER — and the stand-aside AVOIDED A LOSS. The card required SPX to RECLAIM 7,450 (the zero-gamma flip) and HOLD it ~30 minutes — the hold-filter was the entire entry, separating a real gap-and-go from a gap-and-fade. What happened: the +2.4% Nasdaq-futures gap faded into the cash open. SPX opened 7,404.91, rallied only to 7,419.08 (~31 pts SHORT of the 7,450 trigger), got rejected there and faded to a 7,323.50 low before closing 7,357.49 (-0.01%). The 7,450 reclaim never came, so by the card's terms the trade never existed; the desk stood aside. Unlike June 10/24 (no-triggers whose theses paid from the sidelines), this one AVOIDED a loss: the bullish thesis was WRONG (no gap-and-go — the index never cleared the flip and closed below its own open), so a 7,475 call bought on a loose entry would have expired worthless. The hold-filter demanded a confirmation the gap never delivered. Mechanism footnote: a premarket futures gap is not a cash-session reclaim — and the most bullish catalyst stack of the month (soft PCE + MU blowout + QCOM) STILL couldn't carry the flip, because the mega-cap memory-cost selloff (AAPL -6.1%, MSFT -3.2%) capped the rip. The bullish mirror of the week's lesson: no confirmation, no trade, in either direction. Desk record 1-2-6 (6th stand-aside; 4 avoided losses, 2 missed winners). NOTE: our own house call 2026-06-23-no-reclaim-7450 graded RIGHT today (the reclaim failed), so the desk correctly declined to fight it — the card said 'if this works our no-reclaim call grades wrong; we take that trade,' and the trade never set up because the no-reclaim call held.
